chunk_summary: Guidelines for selecting first-line antihypertensive agents in pediatric patients with obesity and hypertension, emphasizing weight-based dosing, comorbidity-specific choices, and safety monitoring.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for initiating antihypertensive medication in pediatric patients with obesity.
Identify first-line antihypertensive agents based on comorbidity phenotypes and contraindications.
Apply weight-based dosing guidelines for antihypertensive medications in children.
Recognize the importance of safety monitoring, including serum creatinine and potassium checks.
key_facts: First-line antihypertensive agents include ACE inhibitors (ACEi), ARBs, long-acting calcium channel blockers, and thiazide diuretics.
ACEi/ARB are preferred for hypertension with diabetes, albuminuria, or chronic kidney disease due to renoprotective effects.
Weight-based dosing examples: enalapril 0.08–0.6 mg/kg/day (max 40 mg/day), lisinopril 0.07–0.6 mg/kg/day (max 40 mg/day), losartan 0.7–1.4 mg/kg/day (max 100 mg/day), amlodipine 2.5–10 mg/day.
Beta blockers are not preferred as initial agents in routine pediatric hypertension care.
Monitor serum creatinine and potassium after initiating or escalating ACEi/ARB therapy.
reference_quote: Preferred first-line classes for most children are an ACEi, ARB, long-acting calcium channel blocker, or thiazide diuretic; beta blockers are not preferred as initial agents in routine pediatric hypertension care.
